metadata.title: Compound Interest — The Exponential Cliff
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06–B08 slates · B09 outro

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: The difference between four percent and ten percent sounds small — six percentage points. Over thirty years it's a six-times multiplier. That gap is not arithmetic, it's exponential, and most people can't feel it until they see it drawn. This video builds the simulator, then shows the cliff.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 4% vs 10% over 30 years — a '6x gap' warning; no terminal yet

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The difference between four percent and ten percent sounds small — six percentage points. Over thirty years it's a six-times multiplier. That gap is not arithme

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to build the simulator. In the terminal: compute compound interest A equals P times open-paren one plus r over n close-paren to the power n-t, for P equals one thousand and three rates — four, seven, and ten percent — monthly compounding over thirty years. Print three formatted tables. No external libraries.

props.command: claude "Write a Python script: compute compound interest A = P*(1+r/n)^(nt) for P=1000 and three rates r=[0.04,0.07,0.10] with monthly compounding (n=12) over t=[0,5,10,15,20,25,30] years. Print three formatted tables. No external libraries."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. One line does everything — A equals P times open-paren one plus r over n close-paren to the n-t. The compounding frequency n equals twelve is what converts annual rate to monthly growth. Everything downstream is just formatting.

props.code:
# compound_interest.py  —  Claude Code output

def compound(P, r, n, t):
    return P * (1 + r/n)**(n*t)  # compound interest formula

rates = [0.04, 0.07, 0.10]
years = [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30]
n = 12  # monthly compounding
P = 1000

for r in rates:
    print(f'\nRate: {r*100:.0f}%')
    for t in years:
        print(f'  t={t:2d}yr: ${compound(P,r,n,t):,.0f}')

beats.narration_text: Run it. Three curves, same thousand dollars, same thirty years. Four percent lands at thirty-three hundred — a decent return. Seven percent: eighty-one hundred. Then ten percent hits nineteen thousand eight hundred — nearly twenty thousand. That's not a line getting steeper; it's a cliff appearing.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: three compound interest curves (4% green, 7% orange, 10% crimson) over 30 years with doubling markers and end labels showing the 6x gap

beats.narration_text: The tables are right but they don't show the doubling times. Iterate: update the script — add a column showing the year each investment first doubles, and verify against the Rule of Seventy-Two.

props.command: claude "update compound_interest.py: add a column showing the first year the investment doubles. Verify against Rule of 72 (double_yr ≈ 72/rate_percent). Print comparison."

beats.role_note: slate — fill with screen recording or Manim
beats.narration_text: The doubling column lands exactly on the Rule of Seventy-Two predictions — eighteen years at four percent, ten at seven, seven at ten. A formula from the sixteenth century, confirmed by the simulation.
beats.visual_intent: Updated table output showing first-doubling year column alongside Rule of 72 predictions for each rate

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The doubling column lands exactly on the Rule of Seventy-Two predictions — eighteen years at four percent, ten at seven, seven at ten. A formula from the sixtee

beats.narration_text: So the machine showed what arithmetic hides: four versus ten percent is not an additive difference — it's a factor of six over a working lifetime. The exponential cliff is real, and you can see exactly where it steepens.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: '4% → $3,310 vs 10% → $19,837 — same 30 years, 6x apart'; show the two curves side by side

build.suggested: suggested prompt: So the machine showed what arithmetic hides: four versus ten percent is not an additive difference — it's a factor of six over a working lifetime. The exponenti

beats.narration_text: Your move: run your own numbers — your actual savings rate, your realistic return. Then try continuous compounding, where n goes to infinity, and watch the gap with monthly compounding collapse to almost nothing above n equals twelve.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) plug in your own P and r, 2) try continuous compounding e^(rt) vs monthly

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Your move: run your own numbers — your actual savings rate, your realistic return. Then try continuous compounding, where n goes to infinity, and watch the gap

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
